data.title: Japanese investors dump foreign stocks, pile into bonds in July as yen weakens
data.description:
Japanese investors sold foreign stocks for a third straight month in July, taking profits after a steep rally left valuations stretched, while channelling large sums into higher-yielding foreign bonds as a weakened yen boosted returns.

According to data from Japan's Ministry of Finance released on Friday, Japanese investors withdrew approximately 536.4 billion yen ($3.64 billion) from foreign equities in the month.

They had sold 1.99 trillion yen worth of foreign stocks in the previous month.

Meanwhile, Japanese investors snapped up 3.63 trillion yen worth of foreign bonds in July, marking a third monthly net purchase.

The yen < JPY=> dipped approximately 4.5% against the dollar last month, the sharpest for a month since December 2024.

Trust accounts (pension funds) sold foreign stocks and bought long-term bonds on a net basis for a third straight month. They divested a net 1.52 trillion yen worth of foreign equities and bought a net 419.6 billion yen worth of long-term bonds.

July's net foreign equity investments by Japanese bankers, investment trust management companies and insurers stood at 445.5 billion yen, 333.5 billion yen and 207.1 billion yen, respectively.

Overseas bond markets received 3.82 trillion yen worth of Japanese investments in long-term bonds. Short-term bills, however, logged a net 196.6 billion yen divestment.

Separate data from the Bank of Japan showed that Japanese market participants bought a net 5.73 trillion yen worth of U.S. bonds in the first half of the year, down from net purchases worth 6.4 trillion yen, a year ago.

They also invested 2.37 trillion yen in European debt securities in the first half. French and German bonds received a significant 702 billion yen and 494 billion yen worth of Japanese net investments.

($1 = 147.3800 yen)

data.title: India's Adani Enterprises set to be biggest Nifty 50 loser this week
data.description:
** Adani Enterprises falls as much as 3.8% to 2,164.8 rupees, a four-month low

** Stock down 6.6% so far this week; set to be worst hit among Nifty 50 stocks

** Co posted lower Q1 profit on July, down ~14% since

** Broader market pressure due to U.S. trade tensions adding to ADEL's losses this week

** YTD, stock down 14% vs Nifty's ~3% rise

data.title: Slovakia Industrial Output Falls at Softer Pace
data.description:
Industrial production in Slovakia fell by 3.6% year-on-year in June 2025, easing from a 4.1% drop in the previous month.

This was primarily driven by a softer contraction in manufacturing output (-2.3% vs -3.8% in May), particularly in the production of rubber and plastic products, and other non-metallic mineral products (-0.6% vs -4.7%), electrical equipment (-2.2% vs -10.4%), and machinery and equipment (-8% vs -13.1%).

Meanwhile, output declined in mining and quarrying, decreasing by 1.6% from a 4.1% gain in the previous month, while production dropped further for electricity and gas (-14.8% vs -7.4%).

On a monthly basis, industrial production fell by 1.8%, easing from a 2.8% drop in the previous period.

data.title: U.S. Futures Up, European Stocks Mixed as Markets Digest Trump's New Tariffs
data.description:
U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were up 0.2% and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.2%. Changes in futures do not necessarily predict movements after the opening bell.

In Europe, the Stoxx Europe 600 climbed 0.2% in morning trading. Bechtle added 10.6% and Lotus Bakeries rose 7%. On the other hand, TBC Bank Group lost 11.1%, and Muenchener Rueckversicher fell 7.2%. The FTSE 100 added 0.2%. Other stocks in Europe were mixed as France's CAC 40 increased 0.3% and Germany's DAX dropped 0.2%.

The Wall Street Journal Dollar Index climbed 0.1% to 95.33.

In commodities, Brent crude dropped 0.2% to $66.33 a barrel, and WTI crude fell 0.2% to $63.73 a barrel. The European benchmark price for natural gas, the Dutch futures contract TTF, was down 0.3% to 32.86 euros a megawatt hour.

The German 10-year Bund yield climbed by 2 basis points to 2.649% from 2.633%, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury held steady at 4.257%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions.

Stocks in Asia were mixed as Japan's Nikkei 225 index gained 1.9%, whereas Hong Kong's Hang Seng declined 1%. China's benchmark Shanghai Composite dropped 0.1%.

-This article was automatically generated by MarketWatch.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: UK PM Starmer says Israel's decision on Gaza City is wrong
data.description:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer on Friday said Israel's decision to take control of Gaza City was wrong and urged the Israeli government to reconsider.

"The Israeli Government’s decision to further escalate its offensive in Gaza is wrong, and we urge it to reconsider immediately," he said in a statement.

"This action will do nothing to bring an end to this conflict or to help secure the release of the hostages. It will only bring more bloodshed."

data.title: Thyssenkrupp investors to vote on defence spin-off
data.description:
Shareholders in Thyssenkrupp are scheduled to vote on a planned spin-off of its defence division on Friday, as a radical shift in European spending on defence has raised expectations its value could surge.

In response to the stance of U.S. President Donald Trump, NATO's European members are increasing their defence budgets, which has driven up the shares of companies in the sector.

The order book of Thyssenkrupp's division TKMS - which makes submarines, frigates and develops mine-sweeping technologies - has swollen to more than 18 billion euros ($21 billion) from 11.7 billion at the end of September last year.

That has in turn boosted shares of its parent, which have more than doubled year-to-date.

Thyssenkrupp plans to spin off 49% of TKMS to its existing shareholders while keeping the remaining stake.

The general meeting will start at 0800 GMT and a vote will be held later in the day following an exchange between management and investors, which include the Alfried Krupp von Bohlen and Halbach foundation, Thyssenkrupp's largest shareholder.

Analysts expect the spin-off and separate listing to take place later this year, although Thyssenkrupp has yet to specify the timing.

Last month, Berlin reached a preliminary agreement with Thyssenkrupp that guarantees it will retain influence at TKMS as the government seeks to keep a certain level of control over strategic defence assets.

($1 = 0.8583 euros)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0737 GMT - DBS's growth should be sustainable in the medium term, Maybank IB's Thilan Wickramasinghe says in a note. The Singapore lender generated above-market-trend growth across all pillars in 2Q, which he expects to be supported by DBS's scale and strong management execution. DBS says it can raise base dividends while rolling out its capital returns program until 2027, which provides yield clarity, he adds. He expects DBS's early deployment of artificial intelligence and new verticals such as digital assets to further consolidate its lead over rivals. He raises his 2025-2027 earnings forecasts by 5%-11%, based on his more positive operational assumptions. Maybank IB lifts its rating to buy from hold and raises its target to S$56.15 from S$45.26. Shares are 1.4% higher at S$50.45. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0725 GMT - Jungheinrich flagged a continued subdued environment in its core European markets and heightened competition in its second-quarter results, which suggest a recently launched restructuring was a good decision, Jefferies analysts say. "We see the restructuring as the right strategic actions given limited growth in Europe and increased competitive pressure, with the majority of effects seen in 2027," the analysts write in a research note. The German forklift maker reported second-quarter results that were slightly better than it indicated when it cut its outlook twice last month, Jefferies says. Orders returned to growth as expected despite weakness in Europe, but cash flow was on the weaker side, the analysts say. Shares fall 3.1%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0724 GMT - European luxury companies could start to see a rebound after weak results, Deutsche Bank analysts write in a research note. The second-quarter earnings season didn't throw up many surprises, with most luxury brands seeing a sequential slowdown from the previous three months, the analysts say. Companies that are more exposed to tourism and to lower-income consumers faced greater challenges, they add. Despite current difficulties, Deutsche Bank expects a sequential sales improvement in the second half. "The investor view on the sector remains largely negative but there are early signs of this starting to change," DB says. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0718 GMT - NN Group's first-half solvency 2 ratio of 208% will excite investors, KBW analyst Michele Ballatore writes. The Dutch insurance and asset-management group's solvency 2 result solidly beat expectations, he adds. In the absence of any material shocks in the second half of the year, an upgraded share buyback could be closer than expected, he adds. Elsewhere, NN Group's operating capital generation beat consensus views by 7%, driven by a better-than-expected performance in its NL Life division, he adds. Shares trade up 1% at 61.46 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0717 GMT - Airbus delivered 373 commercial aircraft in the first seven months of the year, down 7% on year, a figure that is still far from its full-year target of 820 aircraft, RBC Europe analysts say. To meet this target, the European planemaker will need to deliver an average of 89 aircraft a month until December, they say. The group ended the second quarter with 60 A320 gliders--aircraft without engines. Assuming it has delivered those by year-end, then Airbus would need to deliver 77 aircraft a month to meet the full-year target of 820, the analysts add. This monthly amount is achievable for Airbus, they say. Shares are down 0.1% at 177.18 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0706 GMT - Airbus' 67 aircraft deliveries in July exceeded latest press expectations, Deutsche Bank's analyst Christophe Menard says. The European planemaker increased its deliveries of the A320 aircraft family to 54 units last month, in what Menard calls a reassuring recovery. Airbus only received three net orders in July, after a customer canceled one for an A350-F aircraft, Menard notes. Deutsche Bank raises its target price to 186 euros from 185 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0706 GMT - Airbus announced 67 airliner deliveries in July, bringing the year-to-date total to 373, which is still behind the average, Citi says in a note to investors. However, this does represent a slight catch-up versus June deliveries, it says. Airbus also had 60 so-called gliders--aircraft built but awaiting engines--below Citi's estimate of 65 gliders at the end of July, it adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0705 GMT - Munich Re's 5% cut to its revenue guidance for the year will lead to a 2% drop in consensus estimates, RBC Capital Markets says in a note. The German reinsurer now expects its insurance revenue to come in at 62 billion euros, against 64 billion euros previously due to a lower expected result in its reinsurance division on foreign exchange fluctuations. Elsewhere, Munich Re backed its 6 billion euro net profit guidance after it confirmed its prereleased second-quarter net profit beat. It flagged strong earnings momentum across all business segments, which has translated into high consensus expectations, analyst Mandeep Jagpal and associate Sarah Chong write. "The stock has been very strong coming into these results, up 8% over the last week, partly due to sector rotation, we believe," they add. Shares fall 6%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0623 GMT - KT&G appears to have room to improve shareholder returns, Nomura analyst Siwoo Kim writes in a note. The South Korean tobacco company plans to monetize non-core assets for additional share buybacks and aggressive dividend payouts, Kim says. The company is seen as offsetting sluggishness in the domestic tobacco market with higher average selling prices and bigger volumes for products in overseas markets, he notes. New factories in Kazakhstan and Indonesia could help KT&G improve its profit margin on lower production costs, he adds. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises its target price by 38% to 161,000 won. Shares are 4.8% higher at 145,100 won. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0615 GMT - Riverstone Holdings retains a bull in RHB Research which sees the gloves manufacturer in a "sweet spot." The company is benefiting from a robust pickup in global semiconductor sales in 2025, which is estimated to grow 15.4%, according to World Semiconductor Trade Statistics organization. Cleanroom gloves manufactured by companies such as Riverstone are essential in semiconductor chip manufacturing to prevent contamination and ensure product quality. However, RHB cuts its earnings forecasts for Riverstone by 13% for 2025, 12% for 2026, and 13% for 2027 after the company's 2Q results were below expectations. It trims the stock's target price to S$0.85 from S$0.95 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 4.8% lower at S$0.69. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0610 GMT - Shenzhou International Group will likely absorb just 2 to 3 percentage points of the U.S. tariff burden, Morningstar analyst Ivan Su writes in a note. He says the new 20% tariff on Vietnamese imports is unlikely to heavily affect the earnings outlook of the Chinese apparel maker, which has factories in Vietnam. Only about 16% of Shenzhou's revenue comes from the U.S., and both its revenue and net profit are on track for double-digit growth this year, he says. Despite softer demand from Nike, Shenzhou continues to receive strong orders from Adidas, Uniqlo and Puma, Morningstar adds. The brokerage maintains its HK$110.00 fair-value estimate on the company. Shares are last at HK$56.45. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0606 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening slightly higher with IG calling the OMXS30 up 0.2% at around 2599. President Trump nominated economic council head Stephen Miran as a temporary member of the Federal Reserve's Board of Governors. Fed member Christopher Waller appears to be the clear favorite to succeed Fed Chair Powell next year, SEB senior economist Johan Javeus writes. "On geopolitics, we note that Trump says he can meet Putin next week, even though Putin has not yet agreed to allow Zelenskiy to participate." The Nikkei is sharply higher after good reports in the tech sector and hopes of easing trade tensions with the U.S., while Hong Kong is lower and Shanghai flat, Javeus says. Equity futures are pointing higher in Europe and the U.S. OMXS30 closed at 2593.76, OMXN40 at 2219.01 and OBX at 1537. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: DBS's Growth Likely to Be Sustainable — Market Talk
data.description: DBS's growth should be sustainable in the medium term, Maybank IB's Thilan Wickramasinghe says in a note. The Singapore lender generated above-market-trend growth across all pillars in 2Q, which he expects to be supported by DBS's scale and strong management execution. DBS says it can raise base dividends while rolling out its capital returns program until 2027, which provides yield clarity, he adds. He expects DBS's early deployment of artificial intelligence and new verticals such as digital assets to further consolidate its lead over rivals. He raises his 2025-2027 earnings forecasts by 5%-11%, based on his more positive operational assumptions. Maybank IB lifts its rating to buy from hold and raises its target to S$56.15 from S$45.26. Shares are 1.4% higher at S$50.45. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Austria Industrial Output Rises in June
data.description:
Industrial production in Austria grew by 0.6% year-on-year in June 2025, following an upwardly revised 1.1% increase in the previous month.

Production grew significantly for consumer non-durables (6.3% vs 3.1% in May) and intermediate goods (6.1% vs 2.2%), while capital goods rebounded (0.6% vs -0.7%).

Additionally, production fell at a softer pace for consumer durables (-9.6% vs -11%).

Meanwhile, output declined further for energy (-7.9% vs -4.7%).

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, industrial output fell by 0.2% in June, easing from a downwardly revised 0.4% decline in the preceding period.
